title.english: Kaitiakitanga at Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Guardianship of Our River
title.teReoMāori: Te Kaitiakitanga i Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Tiakina tō tātou Awa

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the local pūrākau (legend) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Stream of the Bellbird), which flows through our rohe. According to Ngāti Kahungunu tradition, the awa was named by the ancestress Hineraukatauri, who transformed into a bellbird to guide her people to pure water sources. Students will explore how this pūrākau informs contemporary kaitiakitanga practices and te reo Māori environmental terminology specific to our awa.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 te reo Māori terms related to freshwater ecosystems (e.g., kōawa - tributary, pūwai - spring, paru - sediment) in spoken and written context
Explain the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its connection to kaitiakitanga using compound sentences in te reo Māori
Create a simple environmental assessment of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using te reo Māori observation frameworks (tiaki, tirotiro, aromatawai)
Propose one practical kaitiaki action for the awa using appropriate te reo Māori terminology and cultural protocols

activities.title: Whakarongo: He Pūrākau mō Te Awa
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua (or recording) share the pūrākau of Hineraukatauri and Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using graphic organizers, they identify key te reo Māori vocabulary (awa, manu, wai māori, kaitiaki) and draft 3 questions in te reo about the story.

activities.title: Titiro: Te Aromatawai Taiao
activities.description: Guided visit to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using customized observation sheets in te reo Māori, students conduct a mini-environmental assessment (tirotiro) noting: he aha ngā mea pai? (what is good?), he aha ngā mea kino? (what is bad?). They photograph/video evidence using tablets.

activities.title: Kōrero: He Whakawhiti Kōrero
activities.description: In pairs, students analyze their observations and compare them to the values in the pūrākau. They prepare and record a short dialogue (1-2 minutes) in te reo Māori proposing whether the awa is being cared for in a way Hineraukatauri would approve, using evidence from their tirotiro.

activities.title: Tuhituhi: He Mahere Kaitiaki
activities.description: Using a template, each student writes a simple kaitiaki action plan (5-7 sentences) in te reo Māori for the awa. The plan must include one specific action (e.g., planting harakeke, organizing a clean-up), use vocabulary from the unit, and explain how it connects to the pūrākau.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hōri Reti (local expert on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku pūrākau)
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku - physical site visit and water testing kits from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Te Papakupu o Te Marautanga o Aotearoa (online Māori dictionary) for vocabulary support
'He Puna Auaha: He Pukapuka Whakaahua mō ngā Taonga o Te Taiao' (photographic book of local environmental terms)

tasks: Completed te reo Māori observation sheet from the awa visit
Recorded dialogue proposing the health status of the awa
Written kaitiaki action plan in te reo Māori

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Whakaatu - Presenting, Level 4: Present information, ideas, and opinions... using language suitable to the audience and purpose
Te Reo Māori: Pānui - Reading, Level 4: Understand and use information and ideas from texts... with some confidence
Tikanga-ā-Iwi: Identity, Culture, and Organisation: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Hauora: Healthy Communities and Environments: Level 4: Investigate and describe ways in which the physical environment and people's actions... affect one another
